name: Smartphone
description: Portable multifunctional computing and communication device.
longDescription: A smartphone integrates telecommunication, computing, sensing, and application execution into a handheld device, enabling digital communication, media consumption, and productivity applications.

historicalContext.summary: Emerged from convergence of mobile phones and PDAs.

chronology.context: Touchscreen-based smartphone architectures became dominant.

relationships.description: Relies on battery assembly process for energy storage systems.

name: Lithium Battery Assembly
description: Manufacturing process for assembling lithium-ion battery cells.
longDescription: A multi-stage electrochemical assembly process involving electrode preparation, cell stacking, electrolyte filling, and sealing under controlled conditions.

historicalContext.summary: Evolved with demand for portable electronics and EVs.

chronology.context: Sony commercialized lithium-ion technology.

specific.transformationLogic: electrochemical energy storage assembly under controlled environment

relationships.description: Provides energy storage components for smartphones.

name: Surface Mount Technology (SMT)
description: Technique for mounting electronic components directly onto PCB surfaces.
longDescription: SMT is a highly automated electronics assembly technique enabling miniaturization and high-density circuit design through pick-and-place machines and reflow soldering.

historicalContext.summary: Replaced through-hole assembly in modern electronics.

chronology.context: Driven by demand for smaller electronics.

relationships.description: Enables automated battery electronics assembly.

name: Precision Manufacturing Capability
description: Organizational capability to manufacture high-tolerance components.
longDescription: Represents the ability of a production system to achieve micron-level tolerances in fabrication processes using advanced machinery and quality control systems.

historicalContext.summary: Developed alongside CNC machining and semiconductor fabrication.

chronology.context: Enabled repeatable precision manufacturing.

relationships.description: Supports SMT component placement precision.

name: Semiconductor Fab Plant
description: Industrial facility for manufacturing semiconductor chips.
longDescription: A semiconductor fabrication plant is a highly controlled industrial environment used to manufacture integrated circuits using photolithography and chemical processing.

historicalContext.summary: Evolved with the rise of integrated circuits.

chronology.context: Enabled mass production of integrated circuits.

relationships.description: Provides infrastructure for SMT-enabled electronics assembly.

name: Global Smartphone Market
description: Global economic market for smartphones.
longDescription: Represents global supply-demand dynamics, pricing structures, competition, and consumer adoption patterns for smartphones.

historicalContext.summary: Rapid expansion after smartphone adoption in 2000s.

chronology.context: Market expansion driven by app ecosystems.

relationships.description: Defines competitive environment for smartphone products.

name: Electronics Assembly Stage
description: Stage in the value chain where electronic components are assembled.
longDescription: This stage includes PCB assembly, component integration, and system testing prior to product distribution.

historicalContext.summary: Standardized in modern electronics manufacturing pipelines.

chronology.context: Shift to specialized EMS providers.

relationships.description: Includes battery assembly as a subprocess.

name: Rare Earth Supply Constraint
description: Constraint arising from limited availability of rare earth materials.
longDescription: Represents supply-side limitations affecting production of electronics due to geopolitical concentration of rare earth element mining and refining.

historicalContext.summary: Increasing relevance due to geopolitical supply concentration.

chronology.context: Highlighted global dependency risks.

relationships.description: Limits availability of materials for battery production.

references.title: Rare Earth Supply Chains
references.description: Analysis of global rare earth dependencies.

name: ISO Quality Standards Body
description: Institution responsible for defining international quality standards.
longDescription: Represents formal standardization bodies such as ISO that define quality, safety, and interoperability standards for global industries.

historicalContext.summary: Established to harmonize international industrial standards.

chronology.context: Created to unify global standards.

relationships.description: SMT processes comply with ISO manufacturing standards.

name: Just-in-Time Manufacturing Strategy
description: Production strategy focused on minimizing inventory and optimizing flow.
longDescription: Just-in-time manufacturing reduces waste by synchronizing production with demand, minimizing inventory holding costs and improving responsiveness.

chronology.context: Formalization of JIT principles.

relationships.description: Implemented within electronics assembly stages.

name: Silicon Wafers
description: Thin slices of semiconductor material used as the substrate for integrated circuits.
longDescription: Base substrate in semiconductor manufacturing enabling integrated circuit fabrication via lithographic processes.
historicalContext: Core enabling material in post-1970s semiconductor industrialization.

name: Integrated Circuits
description: Electronic circuits integrated onto semiconductor substrates.
longDescription: Miniaturized circuits embedding transistors and interconnects on silicon wafers enabling computation and signal processing.
historicalContext: Central artifact of the microelectronics revolution beginning in the 1960s.

name: Printed Circuit Boards
description: Boards that mechanically support and electrically connect electronic components.
longDescription: Layered fiberglass and copper structures providing interconnect architecture for electronic systems.
historicalContext: Standardized in mid-20th century electronics manufacturing.

name: Microprocessors
description: Single-chip central processing units for computing systems.
longDescription: Integrated computational engines combining ALU, control unit, and registers on a single semiconductor die.
historicalContext: Emerged in the 1970s as scaling of integrated circuits enabled CPU-on-chip designs.

name: Memory Modules
description: Circuit boards containing memory chips for data storage.
longDescription: Volatile and non-volatile memory assemblies used in computing systems for temporary and persistent data storage.

name: Capacitors
description: Passive components that store electrical energy in an electric field.
longDescription: Electronic components that store and release electrical energy, used in filtering and signal stabilization.
historicalContext: Fundamental component since early electrical engineering.

name: Resistors
description: Components that implement electrical resistance in circuits.
longDescription: Passive electronic components used to control current flow and divide voltage in electrical systems.
historicalContext: Foundational component in analog and digital electronics.

name: Smartphones
description: Portable devices combining mobile communication and computing.
longDescription: Integrated consumer electronics platform combining telecommunications, computation, sensors, and software ecosystems.
historicalContext: Converged device category emerging post-2007 smartphone revolution.

name: Laptops
description: Portable personal computers with clamshell form factor.
longDescription: General-purpose computing devices integrating CPU, memory, storage, and I/O systems into a portable architecture.
historicalContext: Mainstream personal computing platform since the 1990s.

name: Tablets
description: Touchscreen mobile computing devices.
longDescription: Flat mobile computing platforms optimized for touch interaction and lightweight computing tasks.
historicalContext: Popularized after 2010 as a new mobile computing form factor.

name: Land
description: A finite physical resource consisting of terrestrial surface area and associated geographic attributes that can be used for production, habitation, infrastructure, extraction, conservation, and economic activities.
longDescription: Land is a foundational factor of production providing physical space and location-specific characteristics that enable economic activity. Its value derives from natural attributes, accessibility, legal rights, infrastructure connectivity, environmental conditions, and proximity to markets. Land serves as the substrate upon which agriculture, industry, housing, transportation systems, energy infrastructure, and natural resource extraction are organized.

historicalContext.summary: Land has historically been one of the primary factors of production and a central determinant of economic power, political authority, and wealth creation.

chronology.context: Permanent settlement and agriculture establish land as a key productive asset.
Land ownership becomes the basis of political and economic organization in many societies.
Urbanization and industrialization increase the importance of industrial and commercial land.
Land increasingly becomes a tradable asset integrated into financial and real estate markets.

relationships.description: Provides the physical base required for agricultural production.
Provides access to subsurface mineral resources.
Economic utilization typically depends upon legally defined property rights.
Land value and productivity are enhanced by transportation infrastructure.
Often functions jointly with water resources in productive activities.

metadata.sourceReference: Classical Economics, Economic Geography, Resource Economics

references.title: The Wealth of Nations
references.description: Classical treatment of land as a factor of production.

references.title: Principles of Economics
references.description: Discussion of land, rent, and factor markets.

references.title: Economic Geography
references.description: Analysis of location, land use, and spatial economic activity.

name: Land Market
description: A market system through which rights over land are allocated, exchanged, leased, valued, and transferred among economic actors.
longDescription: The land market is an institutional and economic mechanism governing the exchange of land-related rights. It encompasses transactions involving ownership, leasing, development rights, mineral rights, and other legally recognized claims over land. Because land is immobile, heterogeneous, and location-specific, the land market is typically composed of numerous specialized submarkets differentiated by geography, land use, legal regime, and economic function.

historicalContext.summary: Land markets emerged alongside formal property rights systems and evolved into complex institutional mechanisms for allocating access to land and land-based economic opportunities.

chronology.context: Civilizations developed formal mechanisms for assigning and transferring land rights.
Land allocation became embedded within hierarchical political and economic structures.
Land increasingly became a tradable economic asset.
Specialized land markets emerged for residential, industrial, commercial, and agricultural uses.

relationships.description: Allocates and exchanges rights associated with land resources.
Requires legally defined property rights and enforcement mechanisms.
Serves as a parent category for agricultural land markets.
Serves as a parent category for industrial land markets.
Serves as a parent category for residential land markets.
Serves as a parent category for commercial land markets.
Land values and market activity are strongly influenced by transportation infrastructure.

metadata.sourceReference: Urban Economics, Real Estate Economics, Economic Geography, Institutional Economics

references.title: Urban Economics
references.description: Analysis of land markets, location choice, and spatial allocation.

references.title: Real Estate Economics
references.description: Study of land valuation, transactions, and property rights.

references.title: Institutional Economics
references.description: Role of property rights and governance structures in land allocation.

name: Agricultural Land Market
description: Market for the allocation, sale, lease, and valuation of land used primarily for agricultural production.

historicalContext.summary: Agricultural land markets emerged with formalized property rights and commercial agriculture.

relationships.description: Specialized segment of the land market.

references.title: Agricultural Economics
references.description: Analysis of agricultural land allocation and valuation.

name: Residential Land Market
description: Market for land used for housing and residential development.

historicalContext.summary: Residential land markets developed alongside urbanization.

relationships.description: Specialized segment of the land market.

name: Commercial Land Market
description: Market for land supporting retail, office, and commercial service activities.

historicalContext.summary: Commercial land markets emerged with urban commerce and business districts.

relationships.description: Specialized segment of the land market.

name: Industrial Land Market
description: Market for land used in manufacturing, logistics, warehousing, and industrial production.

historicalContext.summary: Industrial land markets expanded during industrialization.

relationships.description: Specialized segment of the land market.

name: Intergovernmental Org
description: Organization composed primarily of sovereign states, established by treaty.

name: Non-Governmental Org

name: Insurgent Group

name: Terrorist Network

name: Autonomous Region

name: Religious Entity

name: Market
description: A socially constituted institutional structure that coordinates voluntary exchange among economic agents through mechanisms of price formation, allocation, competition, and contractual interaction.
longDescription: A market is a synontic institutional configuration composed of agents, exchange protocols, property rights, contractual rules, information flows, and allocation mechanisms. Markets facilitate the discovery of prices, coordinate decentralized economic activity, transmit information regarding relative scarcity, and enable the exchange of goods, services, assets, labor, and other economic objects. Markets are emergent social structures sustained through repeated participation, legal enforcement, norms, and shared expectations. They are neither individual transactions nor specific organizations, but institutional environments within which such activities occur.

historicalContext.summary: Markets have existed in various forms throughout human history, ranging from local barter arrangements to highly complex global financial and digital marketplaces.

chronology.context: Emergence of organized exchange among agricultural and urban settlements.
Formal analysis of markets as coordination mechanisms in political economy.
Markets modeled through supply, demand, and marginal utility.
Formalization of market mechanisms using game theory and mechanism design.
Expansion of algorithmically mediated and globally connected markets.

specific.realityDescription: An emergent institutional structure governing exchange relationships among economic agents.

relationships.description: Markets are constituent institutional structures of economic systems.
Markets depend upon stable property rights definitions.
Market exchange relies on contractual arrangements.
Prices function as key coordination signals within markets.
Markets emerge from interactions among economic agents.
Markets provide the institutional environment for exchange.
Markets enable competitive interaction among participants.

references.title: Market Institution
references.description: Institutional arrangement enabling economic exchange and coordination.

references.title: Price System
references.description: Mechanism through which markets coordinate decentralized decisions.

name: Productivity
description: A state descriptor expressing the relationship between outputs generated by a productive system and the inputs consumed in production.

specific.realityDescription: A variable aspect of a productive system state describing output-input relationships.

relationships.description: Labor productivity is a specialization of productivity.
TFP is a specialization of productivity.
MFP is a specialization of productivity.

name: Labor Productivity
description: Productivity measure relating output to labor input.

specific.realityDescription: A productivity aspect measured relative to labor inputs.

relationships.description: Specialized productivity measure based on labor.

name: Total Factor Productivity
description: Productivity measure relating output to a weighted aggregation of production factors.

specific.realityDescription: A productivity aspect not directly attributable to measured factor accumulation.

relationships.description: Specialized productivity measure based on multiple factors.

name: Multifactor Productivity
description: Productivity measure relating output to multiple categories of productive inputs.

specific.realityDescription: A productivity aspect measured relative to aggregated heterogeneous inputs.

relationships.description: Specialized productivity measure based on multiple inputs.

name: Productivity Measurement Procedure
description: A measurement operator used to construct productivity measures from observations of outputs and inputs.

relationships.description: Produces productivity estimates.
Produces productivity indices.

name: Productivity Estimate
description: A concrete estimated value assigned to a productivity variable.

relationships.description: Generated through estimation procedures.
Represents an estimate of productivity.

name: Production Function
description: A representational form expressing a mapping from productive inputs to outputs.

relationships.description: Provides a representational structure used to estimate productivity.
Frequently used to model and explain productivity differences.
